links.context: birth ([[LUK.2:9]]-15), Resurrection ([[MAT.28:5]]-7), and Ascension ([[ACT.1:9]]-11). But angelic

When God the Son became incarnate, this can be called an everlasting theophany. For having assumed human nature, Christ not only manifests God to the world during His earthly life ([[JOH.1:14]]; see also 14:9; [[COL.2:9]]; [[1JN.1:1]]-3), but He ascends into heaven in the same glorified flesh in which He will return at His Second Coming (see [[ACT.1:9]]-11). At the baptism of Christ ([[MAT.3:13]]-17), a further theophany occurs, as all three Persons of the Holy Trinity are made known: the Father in the voice from heaven, the Holy Spirit in the form of a dove, and the Incarnate Son. Hence, the feast day commemorating this event is known as Holy Theophany. On this day the Church sings, 'When Thou, O Lord, wast baptized in the Jordan, the worship of the Trinity was made manifest.' Additionally, at Christ's Transfiguration on Mt. Tabor ([[MAT.17:1]]-9), the Father again is heard, the Holy Spirit is present in the brightness of the cloud, and the Son shines with the gleaming radiance of His Divinity. ### Apocalypticliterature *(after DAN.7:8)*
